content.text: Creating a Support Request

A support request is the formal channel for getting help from Microsoft Azure engineers. Creating one well — with the right issue type, severity, and description — determines how quickly engineers can engage and how fast your problem is resolved. This topic covers the full creation flow, the REST API, and the patterns that produce the fastest resolutions.

attrs.alt: Support request creation flow: issue type → classification → description → severity → submit

content.text: The Five Steps of a Support Request
Whether you use the Azure portal or the REST API, every support request follows the same five-step structure.
Step 1: Issue Type
Issue type determines which team receives the case and what information is collected. The four types are:
Issue Type
Scope
Plan Required
Technical
Service errors, SDK bugs, performance issues, API behavior
Developer, Standard, ProDirect, or Premier
Billing
Invoice disputes, charge questions, payment issues, credit claims
Basic (free)
Service and subscription limits
Quota increases, subscription state, resource limits
Basic (free)
Security
Abuse reports, vulnerability disclosures, compliance data requests
None — dedicated channel
Step 2: Problem Classification

Problem classification narrows the issue to a specific service and scenario. Each service has its own classification tree — for example, App Service classifications cover deployment, connectivity, performance, and SDK usage. The correct classification routes the case to a specialist team and prevents reassignment delays.
Step 3: Describe the Problem

The description is the most important field. Engineers triage blind — the description must tell them what failed, when, what you've already tried, and what the exact error is. A weak description triggers a clarification email that adds 4–24 hours before any real work begins.
Step 4: Severity and Contact Information

Severity drives the response SLA. Contact method (email vs. phone) determines how engineers reach you. For Severity A, always provide a phone number and select phone as the preferred contact method — engineers will call immediately after assignment.
Step 5: Review and Submit

Review all fields before submitting. You cannot change the severity or service classification after submission without reopening a new case. Submit, then note the support ticket ID displayed in the confirmation screen.
Creating a Support Request via REST API
The Microsoft.Support REST API provides full programmatic control over case creation. This is the foundation for on-call automation, CI/CD integrations, and support case dashboards.
Step 1: Enumerate Services and Classifications

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

def list_support_services(name_fragment: str = "") -> list[dict]:
    """List all Azure Support services, optionally filtered by name."""
    url = "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Support/services?api-version=2020-04-01"
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    services = resp.json().get("value", [])
    if name_fragment:
        services = [s for s in services
                    if name_fragment.lower() in s["properties"]["displayName"].lower()]
    return [{"id": s["name"], "name": s["properties"]["displayName"]} for s in services]

def list_problem_classifications(service_id: str, keyword: str = "") -> list[dict]:
    """List problem classifications for a service, optionally filtered by keyword."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Support"
        f"/services/{service_id}/problemClassifications?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    classes = resp.json().get("value", [])
    if keyword:
        classes = [c for c in classes
                   if keyword.lower() in c["properties"]["displayName"].lower()]
    return [{"id": c["name"], "name": c["properties"]["displayName"]} for c in classes]

# Find App Service
app_svcs = list_support_services("App Service")
for svc in app_svcs[:3]:
    print(f"Service: {svc['id']} — {svc['name']}")

# Find connectivity-related classifications
APP_SVC_ID = "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00"
conn_classes = list_problem_classifications(APP_SVC_ID, "connectivity")
for c in conn_classes:
    print(f"  Classification: {c['id']} — {c['name']}")
Step 2: Create the Ticket

import datetime, re

SUBSCRIPTION_ID = "your-subscription-id"

def create_support_ticket(
    title: str,
    description: str,
    service_id: str,
    problem_classification_id: str,
    severity: str,                   # "minimal" | "moderate" | "critical"
    contact_email: str,
    contact_phone: str = "",
    resource_id: str = "",
) -> dict:
    """Create a support ticket and return the ticket metadata."""
    safe_title = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "-", title.lower())[:40]
    ticket_name = f"{safe_title}-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M%S')}"

    contact = {
        "firstName": "Ops",
        "lastName": "Team",
        "primaryEmailAddress": contact_email,
        "preferredContactMethod": "phone" if contact_phone and severity == "critical" else "email",
        "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
        "country": "US",
        "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
    }
    if contact_phone:
        contact["phoneNumber"] = contact_phone

    properties = {
        "title": title,
        "description": description,
        "severity": severity,
        "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}",
        "problemClassificationId": (
            f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}"
            f"/problemClassifications/{problem_classification_id}"
        ),
        "contactDetails": contact,
    }
    if resource_id:
        properties["technicalTicketDetails"] = {"resourceId": resource_id}

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.put(url, json={"properties": properties}, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    return {
        "ticket_name": data["name"],
        "support_ticket_id": data["properties"].get("supportTicketId"),
        "status": data["properties"]["status"],
        "severity": data["properties"]["severity"],
        "url": f"https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Azure_Support/SupportRequestBlade/id/{data['name']}",
    }

ticket = create_support_ticket(
    title="App Service returning 503 for all inbound requests — production",
    description=(
        "All requests to https://api.example.com return HTTP 503 since 14:22 UTC. "
        "App Service Plan: P2v3, 3 instances. All instances show 'stopped' in portal. "
        "No deployment in the last 4 hours. Restart via portal has no effect. "
        "App Service logs: 'Worker process failed to start within the allotted time.'"
    ),
    service_id="0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00",
    problem_classification_id="<classification-id>",
    severity="critical",
    contact_email="oncall@company.com",
    contact_phone="+15555550100",
    resource_id=(
        "/subscriptions/your-sub/resourceGroups/prod-rg"
        "/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/prod-api"
    ),
)
print(f"Created: {ticket['ticket_name']}")
print(f"Portal: {ticket['url']}")
Adding Communications to an Existing Ticket

After a case is created, you can add updates and responses programmatically using the communications API. This is useful for automated systems that need to append diagnostic data collected after the initial case was opened.

def add_ticket_communication(
    subscription_id: str,
    ticket_name: str,
    subject: str,
    body: str,
    sender_email: str,
) -> dict:
    """Append a communication (reply) to an existing support ticket."""
    import datetime, re
    comm_name = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "-", subject.lower())[:30]
    comm_name += f"-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%H%M%S')}"

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"/communications/{comm_name}?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    body_payload = {
        "properties": {
            "subject": subject,
            "body": body,
            "sender": sender_email,
            "communicationType": "web",
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body_payload, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

# Append follow-up diagnostic data collected 10 minutes after opening
add_ticket_communication(
    subscription_id=SUBSCRIPTION_ID,
    ticket_name="app-service-returning-503-for-all-inb-20260701142200",
    subject="Additional diagnostics — boot diagnostics log attached",
    body=(
        "Serial console log shows: 'ASP.NET Core module failed to start process with commandline'\n"
        "App version: 2.1.45 (deployed 10:30 UTC — 4 hours before issue, not 0 hours as stated)\n"
        "Rolling back to 2.1.44 in progress. Will update on outcome."
    ),
    sender_email="oncall@company.com",
)
Querying Support Ticket History

Reviewing historical case data helps identify recurring patterns — the same service failing repeatedly, certain deployment windows producing incidents, or specific regions generating disproportionate case volume.

from collections import Counter
import datetime

def analyze_ticket_history(subscription_id: str, days_back: int = 180) -> dict:
    """Analyse support ticket history to surface recurring issues."""
    cutoff = (
        datetime.datetime.utcnow() - datetime.timedelta(days=days_back)
    ).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets"
        f"?api-version=2020-04-01&$filter=createdDate ge {cutoff}&$top=200"
    )
    tickets = []
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        for t in data.get("value", []):
            p = t["properties"]
            created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(p["createdDate"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
            modified = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(p["modifiedDate"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
            tickets.append({
                "id": t["name"],
                "service": p.get("serviceDisplayName", "Unknown"),
                "classification": p.get("problemClassificationDisplayName", "Unknown"),
                "severity": p["severity"],
                "status": p["status"],
                "resolution_hours": round((modified - created).total_seconds() / 3600, 1),
            })
        url = data.get("nextLink")

    if not tickets:
        return {"total": 0}

    service_counts = Counter(t["service"] for t in tickets)
    severity_counts = Counter(t["severity"] for t in tickets)
    avg_resolution = sum(t["resolution_hours"] for t in tickets) / len(tickets)

    return {
        "total_tickets": len(tickets),
        "period_days": days_back,
        "top_services": service_counts.most_common(5),
        "severity_breakdown": dict(severity_counts),
        "avg_resolution_hours": round(avg_resolution, 1),
        "open_tickets": len([t for t in tickets if t["status"] == "open"]),
    }

report = analyze_ticket_history(SUBSCRIPTION_ID, days_back=90)
print(f"Tickets in last 90 days: {report['total_tickets']}")
print(f"Average resolution: {report['avg_resolution_hours']} hours")
print("Top services by ticket volume:")
for svc, count in report.get("top_services", []):
    print(f"  {svc}: {count} tickets")
Common Mistakes That Delay Resolution
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Wrong service classification
Case reassigned — adds 4–12 hrs
Enumerate /problemClassifications before scripting
Vague description ('it doesn't work')
Clarification round-trip before triage begins
Include resource ID, error text, and steps to reproduce
Severity A without phone contact available
Engineer cannot proceed — case stalls
Always staff the phone number in Severity A cases
Opening a duplicate case while waiting
Splits engineer attention; neither case resolves faster
Add a communication to the existing ticket instead
Not attaching logs at case creation
Engineer requests them — adds 8–24 hrs before they can diagnose
Attach JSON diagnostic files in the creation API call
Selecting 'minimal' severity for a production issue
Joins the wrong queue; response may take 8+ hrs
Match severity honestly to actual business impact
Building a Support Case Library

Teams that support production Azure environments benefit from a pre-built library of case templates — one per common failure scenario. Each template pre-fills the service ID, classification GUID, severity, and a structured description skeleton, reducing case creation to under 60 seconds during incidents.
Template Structure

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import json, datetime, requests, re
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

@dataclass
class SupportCaseTemplate:
    name: str                          # human-readable template name
    service_id: str                    # Microsoft.Support service GUID
    classification_id: str            # problem classification GUID
    default_severity: str             # critical | moderate | minimal
    title_template: str               # may contain {resource} placeholder
    description_template: str         # may contain {resource}, {error}, {timestamp}

    def render_title(self, **kwargs) -> str:
        return self.title_template.format(**kwargs)

    def render_description(self, **kwargs) -> str:
        return self.description_template.format(**kwargs)

# Pre-built templates for common Azure failure patterns
CASE_TEMPLATES: dict[str, SupportCaseTemplate] = {
    "app_service_503": SupportCaseTemplate(
        name="App Service — HTTP 503 / worker process crash",
        service_id="0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00",
        classification_id="32d322a8-acae-202d-e9a9-7371dccf381b",
        default_severity="critical",
        title_template="[CRITICAL] {resource} returning HTTP 503 — production",
        description_template=(
            "Resource: {resource}\n"
            "Error: {error}\n"
            "Started: {timestamp} UTC\n\n"
            "All instances show worker process crash. Restart has no effect. "
            "Last deployment: {last_deploy}. Revenue impact: active."
        ),
    ),
    "aks_node_not_ready": SupportCaseTemplate(
        name="AKS — node(s) NotReady",
        service_id="<aks-service-id>",
        classification_id="<aks-node-class-id>",
        default_severity="critical",
        title_template="[CRITICAL] AKS cluster {resource} — {node_count} nodes NotReady",
        description_template=(
            "Cluster: {resource}\n"
            "NotReady nodes: {node_count}\n"
            "First seen: {timestamp} UTC\n\n"
            "kubectl get nodes output:\n{kubectl_output}\n\n"
            "No recent node pool scaling events. VM provisioning state: succeeded."
        ),
    ),
    "sql_connection_timeout": SupportCaseTemplate(
        name="Azure SQL — connection timeout from app layer",
        service_id="<sql-service-id>",
        classification_id="<sql-conn-class-id>",
        default_severity="moderate",
        title_template="Azure SQL {resource} — intermittent connection timeout from {source}",
        description_template=(
            "Database: {resource}\n"
            "Source: {source}\n"
            "Error: {error}\n"
            "Started: {timestamp} UTC\n\n"
            "Timeout rate: ~{timeout_pct}% of connections. "
            "Connection string uses retry=3. DTU utilisation: {dtu_pct}%."
        ),
    ),
    "quota_increase": SupportCaseTemplate(
        name="Quota increase — VM cores",
        service_id="517f2da6-78fd-0498-4e22-ad26996b1dfc",  # Subscription management
        classification_id="<quota-class-id>",
        default_severity="minimal",
        title_template="Quota increase request — {vm_family} vCPUs in {region}",
        description_template=(
            "Subscription: {subscription_id}\n"
            "Region: {region}\n"
            "VM family: {vm_family}\n"
            "Current limit: {current_limit} vCPUs\n"
            "Requested limit: {requested_limit} vCPUs\n"
            "Business justification: {justification}"
        ),
    ),
}

def open_case_from_template(
    template_key: str,
    subscription_id: str,
    contact_email: str,
    contact_phone: str = "",
    **template_kwargs,
) -> dict:
    """Open a support case from a pre-built template."""
    tmpl = CASE_TEMPLATES[template_key]
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    now = datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    template_kwargs.setdefault("timestamp", now)

    title = tmpl.render_title(**template_kwargs)
    description = tmpl.render_description(**template_kwargs)
    safe = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "-", title.lower())[:35]
    ticket_name = f"{safe}-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M%S')}"

    contact = {
        "firstName": "Ops",
        "lastName": "Team",
        "primaryEmailAddress": contact_email,
        "preferredContactMethod": "phone" if contact_phone and tmpl.default_severity == "critical" else "email",
        "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
        "country": "US",
        "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
    }
    if contact_phone:
        contact["phoneNumber"] = contact_phone

    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": title,
            "description": description,
            "severity": tmpl.default_severity,
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{tmpl.service_id}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{tmpl.service_id}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{tmpl.classification_id}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": contact,
        }
    }
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return {"ticket": ticket_name, "status": resp.json()["properties"]["status"]}

# On-call usage: open App Service 503 case in under 5 lines
result = open_case_from_template(
    template_key="app_service_503",
    subscription_id="your-sub",
    contact_email="oncall@company.com",
    contact_phone="+15555550100",
    resource="prod-api (app service, East US 2)",
    error="Worker process failed to start within allotted time",
    last_deploy="2026-07-01T10:30 UTC (build 2.1.45)",
)
print(f"Opened: {result['ticket']} — {result['status']}")
Integrating Support Cases into PagerDuty / Teams

When a support ticket is created or its status changes, on-call teams need immediate notification. The following pattern polls for status changes and posts updates to a Microsoft Teams channel via an Incoming Webhook.
Closing and Rating a Resolved Ticket

Once Azure resolves your issue, the ticket moves to 'closed' status. Azure sends a survey; completing it improves routing quality for future cases. You can also programmatically mark a ticket as closed if the issue self-resolved before engineer contact.

def close_ticket(
    subscription_id: str,
    ticket_name: str,
    resolution_note: str = "Issue resolved — no further action required.",
) -> dict:
    """Close a support ticket that resolved before engineer engagement."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {credential.get_token('https://management.azure.com/.default').token}"}
    body = {"properties": {"status": "closed"}}
    resp = requests.patch(url, json=body, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()

    # Append resolution note as communication
    add_ticket_communication(
        subscription_id=subscription_id,
        ticket_name=ticket_name,
        subject="Resolved — closing ticket",
        body=resolution_note,
        sender_email="devops@company.com",
    )
    return {"ticket": ticket_name, "status": "closed"}

close_ticket(
    SUBSCRIPTION_ID,
    "app-service-returning-503-for-all-inb-20260701142200",
    "Root cause: application bug in 2.1.45 — rolled back to 2.1.44. Service restored at 15:10 UTC.",
)
Summary

A support request moves through five steps: issue type, problem classification, description, severity and contact, then submission. The REST API (Microsoft.Support) gives full programmatic control — use it to pre-build case creation scripts for on-call scenarios so engineers can open a ticket in under 60 seconds.
Technical cases require a paid support plan; billing and quota cases are available on the free Basic plan.
The problem classification GUID determines which specialist team receives the case — enumerate classifications via the API before scripting.
Description quality is the single biggest factor in resolution time: include resource IDs, exact error text, reproduction steps, and what you've already tried.
For Severity A, select phone as the contact method and provide a number staffed immediately — engineers call within the hour.
Use the communications API to append diagnostic data collected after case creation rather than opening duplicate tickets.
